title: $NLS_DISABLE_NON_GREGORIAN_CALENDAR
text: $NLS_DISABLE_NON_GREGORIAN_CALENDAR Disable support for non-Gregorian calendars, such as Islamic calendar, Persian calendar, and others, to ensure that $date , $datim , and $clock works compatibly with versions prior to Uniface 10.4.03. $NLS_DISABLE_NON_GREGORIAN_CALENDAR { = } { 0 } | { 1 } Arguments 0 —The non-Gregorian calendars are enabled. 1 —The non-Gregorian calendars are disabled. Defaults Assignment file: Any application assignment file Section: [SETTINGS] Default value: 0 Description The $date , $datim , and $clock functions started supporting non-Gregorian calendars from Uniface 10.4.03 onwards. The applications developed before this version that use $nlsformat=”nlslocale” and a non-Gregorian calendar, may observe different behaviors of $date , $datim , and $clock functions when migrated to Uniface 10.4.03. The $NLS_DISABLE_NON_GREGORIAN_CALENDAR setting is only provided for compatibility purposes. The default calendars for the locales listed below are non-Gregorian: ar_SA ckb_IR fa fa_AF fa_IR lrc lrc_IR mzn mzn_IR ps ps_AF th th_TH uz_Arab uz_Arab_AF All other locales, unless explicitly set to non-Gregorian, are by default aligned to the Gregorian calendar. Note: $NLS_DISABLE_NON_GREGORIAN_CALENDAR should be used only for the applications developed before Uniface 10.4.03 that use $nlsformat="nlslocale" and a non-Gregorian calendar. Using this setting is generally discouraged as it limits the local language support. Example: Using $NLS_DISABLE_NON_GREGORIAN_CALENDAR In the following example, the application developed with 10.4.02 is using $nlsformat="nlslocale" and the default calendar for ar_SA is a non-Gregorian calendar. $nlsformat="nlslocale" $nlslocale="ar_SA" When you migrate to Uniface 10.4.03, the return values of $date , $datim , and $clock are different. To ensure compatibility, configure the compatibility switch as follows: Explain this code [SETTINGS] $NLS_DISABLE_NON_GREGORIAN_CALENDAR = 1 Copy Related Links $date Return the current date or convert the argument to the Date data type. $datim Return the system date and time, or convert the argument to the Datetime data type. $clock Return the system time or convert the argument to the Time data type.
